_comment_HOW_TO_USE: This is the ONLY file you edit to add a lesson. Change the 'topic' to any Excel function (SUMIFS, VLOOKUP, etc.). Add or remove exercises in the 'exercises' array. Save the file and refresh the browser to see changes.
_comment_DATASET: Columns: A=Date, B=Vendor, C=Category, D=Department, E=GL_Account, F=Amount. Answer cells start at I2 downward (gap columns G-H keep answers clear of data).
schema: The dataset contains 35 transaction rows in rows 2–37, with no header row conflicts; all range references span F2:F37 for amounts and A2:A37 through E2:E37 for criteria columns. Column A (Date) stores dates as text strings in YYYY-MM-DD format, which means lexicographic string comparison works correctly for >= and <= operators without needing DATEVALUE conversion. Column B (Vendor) contains supplier names such as Deloitte, EY, and PwC; Column C (Category) and Column D (Department) are fully independent columns and will not always share the same value on a given row — a row can have Category='Marketing' while Department='HR'. Column E (GL_Account) holds numeric-looking codes stored as text (e.g., '5001', '5100', '6001', '6100', '7001'), which means wildcard criteria like '5*' perform text-pattern matching and will correctly match any code beginning with the digit 5. Column F (Amount) contains positive numeric dollar values representing individual invoice amounts; it serves as the sum_range in every exercise but can also appear as a criteria_range simultaneously when a numeric threshold filter is needed. SUMIFS always returns 0 — never an error — when no rows satisfy all criteria simultaneously, so a result of 0 must be interpreted as either a genuine zero sum or a misconfigured criterion, not a formula error. Answer cells begin at I2 and continue downward; columns G and H are intentionally empty to visually separate the data table from answer output.
_comment_SCAFFOLD: The AI tutor uses wrongAttempts to escalate hints: (1) clarifying question → (2) concept pointer → (3) parallel example → (4) skeleton with blanks → (4+) full answer only if explicitly asked. Each exercise below has tutorFocus and expectedBehavior fields the tutor uses to target its scaffolding.

exercises.title: Drill 1 — Single text criterion
exercises.concept: One filter column, one text value. The foundational SUMIFS pattern.
exercises.scenario: Column C holds the spending category for each transaction. Column F holds the amount. All 35 rows cover Q3 2024 (Jul–Sep).
exercises.controllerAsk: Your CFO asks: 'What is the total spend on all Marketing-category transactions across the entire Q3 period?'
exercises.tutorFocus: sum_range is F2:F37 (Amount). criteria_range1 is C2:C37 (Category). criteria1 is the text string "Marketing". Common error: swapping sum_range and criteria_range.
exercises.expectedBehavior: A correct formula returns exactly 50700, representing the sum of all Amount values in F2:F37 where the corresponding Category in C2:C37 equals the text string 'Marketing' (case-insensitive). The formula requires exactly one criteria pair: sum_range F2:F37 followed by criteria_range C2:C37 and criteria "Marketing". A result of 0 indicates the criteria_range and sum_range have been swapped (i.e., SUMIFS(C2:C37, F2:F37, "Marketing")), because no numeric amount equals the text 'Marketing'. Any result other than 50700 that is nonzero — such as a partial subtotal — suggests the range does not cover all 35 rows (e.g., stopping at F36 instead of F37) or a misspelled category string.
exercises._hint_mcq: Which range is the sum_range? | A) C2:C37 | B) F2:F37 | C) D2:D37 | D) A2:A37 | correct: B

exercises.title: Drill 2 — Two text criteria (AND logic)
exercises.concept: Multiple text criteria with AND logic. Every criteria_range/criteria pair must match simultaneously.
exercises.scenario: Marketing-category spend appears across three departments: Marketing posts ads (Marketing dept), HR runs job campaigns tagged as Marketing, and Sales buys some marketing tools. You need to isolate only the pure Marketing-department marketing spend.
exercises.controllerAsk: Your CMO asks: 'How much did the Marketing department itself spend on Marketing-category items — exclude HR and Sales marketing charges?'
exercises.tutorFocus: Two criteria pairs needed: (C2:C37, "Marketing") AND (D2:D37, "Marketing"). The key insight is that BOTH conditions must be true on the same row. Indeed (row 10) is Marketing category but HR department — it gets excluded by the second filter.
exercises.expectedBehavior: A correct formula returns exactly 49200, representing only the rows where Category (C2:C37) equals 'Marketing' AND Department (D2:D37) equals 'Marketing' simultaneously on the same row. The formula requires two criteria pairs after the sum_range: (C2:C37, "Marketing") and (D2:D37, "Marketing"). A result of 50700 means the second criteria pair on column D is missing, so the formula is behaving identically to Drill 1 and including cross-departmental rows such as the HR-department marketing charge. A result of 0 means either both criteria ranges are pointing at the same column (e.g., D used twice) or the department string is misspelled; SUMIFS returns 0 on no match, not an error.
exercises._hint_mcq: What is the second criteria_range for this formula? | A) A2:A37 | B) C2:C37 | C) D2:D37 | D) F2:F37 | correct: C

exercises.title: Drill 3 — Date range (>= and <= on same column)
exercises.concept: Two conditions on the SAME column. A date range requires a >= lower bound and a <= upper bound, both pointing at column A.
exercises.scenario: Date filters require TWO criteria on the Date column — a start boundary and an end boundary. Both criteria_range arguments will be A2:A37. Dates in column A are stored in YYYY-MM-DD text format, which sorts correctly for text comparison.
exercises.controllerAsk: Your CFO asks: 'How much did we spend on Marketing-category items specifically during August 2024?'
exercises.tutorFocus: Three criteria pairs: (C2:C37, "Marketing") for category, then (A2:A37, ">=2024-08-01") AND (A2:A37, "<=2024-08-31") for the date range. The key insight: A2:A37 appears twice — once for start boundary, once for end. This is how date ranges work in SUMIFS.
exercises.expectedBehavior: A correct formula returns exactly 16400, representing Marketing-category spend where the Date falls within August 2024 (2024-08-01 through 2024-08-31 inclusive). The formula requires three criteria pairs: (C2:C37, "Marketing"), (A2:A37, ">=2024-08-01"), and (A2:A37, "<=2024-08-31") — note that A2:A37 must appear twice as a criteria_range, once for each date boundary. A result of 50700 means the date criteria pairs are missing entirely, leaving only the Marketing category filter from Drill 1. A result larger than 16400 but less than 50700 typically means only one date boundary is present (either the >= or the <= is missing), causing the formula to sum across multiple months. Because dates are stored as YYYY-MM-DD text, the comparison operators work via string sort order; using a numeric date serial or a differently formatted string will cause a mismatch and return 0.
exercises._hint_mcq: How many times does A2:A37 appear in the formula for an August date range? | A) Once | B) Twice | C) Three times | D) Not at all | correct: B

exercises.title: Drill 4 — Numeric threshold (same column as sum AND criteria)
exercises.concept: A numeric operator (>) as criteria. The Amount column (F) is used TWICE — as sum_range and as a criteria_range for the threshold filter.
exercises.scenario: Auditors want to flag large invoices only. Professional Services vendors (Deloitte, EY, PwC) each submitted multiple invoices at varying sizes. You need to sum only the ones that individually exceeded $10,000.
exercises.controllerAsk: Your internal auditor asks: 'What is the total value of Professional Services invoices where a single invoice exceeded $10,000?'
exercises.tutorFocus: sum_range = F2:F37 (Amount). First criteria pair: (C2:C37, "Professional Services"). Second criteria pair: (F2:F37, ">10000") — note F2:F37 appears TWICE in this formula: as the sum_range at the start, and again as a criteria_range for the threshold. Three invoices qualify: Deloitte Jul $12,500, EY Aug $15,000, Deloitte Sep $11,000.
exercises.expectedBehavior: A correct formula returns exactly 38500, which is the sum of exactly three invoices: Deloitte July ($12,500), EY August ($15,000), and Deloitte September ($11,000), all categorized as 'Professional Services' and each individually exceeding $10,000. The formula requires two criteria pairs: (C2:C37, "Professional Services") and (F2:F37, ">10000"), meaning column F appears three times in the formula — first as the sum_range, then again as a criteria_range for the threshold. A result of the full Professional Services total (larger than 38500) means the numeric threshold criteria pair on F2:F37 is absent, so all Professional Services invoices are being summed regardless of size. A result of 0 most likely indicates the criteria string uses '>10,000' with a comma or '>=10000' with the wrong operator, or that 'Professional Services' is misspelled; SUMIFS returns 0 on no match rather than an error.
exercises._hint_mcq: Which criteria string correctly applies the $10,000 threshold? | A) "10000" | B) ">10000" | C) ">=10000" | D) "<10000" | correct: B

exercises.title: Drill 5 — Wildcard (*) on GL account codes
exercises.concept: Wildcards (* and ?) allow pattern matching. "5*" matches any value starting with 5, regardless of what follows.
exercises.scenario: Column E holds GL account codes. Marketing spend is coded 5001. Travel is coded 5100. Software is 6001, Office is 6100, Professional Services is 7001. Your chart of accounts groups the 5000-range as operational field expenses.
exercises.controllerAsk: Your controller asks: 'What is the combined total for all transactions coded to the GL 5000 range — any account code that starts with 5?'
exercises.tutorFocus: criteria_range1 is E2:E37 (GL_Account). criteria1 is "5*" — the asterisk is a wildcard meaning 'any characters after 5'. This matches both 5001 (Marketing) and 5100 (Travel). The result is Marketing total ($50,700) + Travel total ($6,165) = $56,865. Common error: using "5000" (exact match) instead of "5*" (wildcard).
exercises.expectedBehavior: A correct formula returns exactly 56865, which equals the Marketing GL total (50700, GL code 5001) plus the Travel GL total (6165, GL code 5100) — all transactions whose GL_Account code in E2:E37 begins with the digit 5. The formula requires a single criteria pair: (E2:E37, "5*"), where the asterisk wildcard matches any text starting with '5' regardless of subsequent characters. A result of 0 indicates the wildcard syntax is absent or malformed — for example, using the exact string "5" (no asterisk) matches nothing because no code is a bare '5', and SUMIFS returns 0 on no match. A result of only 50700 means the criterion was written as "5001" (exact match), capturing only Marketing transactions and missing Travel (5100). Including a sum_range of F2:F37 is still required as the first argument even though only one criteria pair follows.
exercises._hint_mcq: Which criteria string matches any GL code starting with 5? | A) "5000" | B) "5???" | C) "5*" | D) ">5000" | correct: C

exercises.title: Conditional Format — Flag Large Invoices
exercises.concept: Conditional formatting highlights cells automatically based on a rule — no formula needed in the answer cell.
exercises.scenario: The finance team wants to visually flag high-value transactions so auditors can spot them at a glance. Column F holds each invoice amount.
exercises.controllerAsk: Select F2:F55, then open Conditional Formatting > Highlight Cells Rules > Greater Than, enter 10000, and choose any red fill. Click Submit when done.
exercises.tutorFocus: This is a UI exercise, not a formula exercise. Select the range F2:F55 first, then apply the Conditional Formatting rule via the Home ribbon or Format menu. The grader checks that a GreaterThan rule with value 10000 exists — the exact colour does not matter.
exercises.expectedBehavior: The exercise passes when a conditional format rule of type GreaterThan with value 10000 has been applied anywhere in the spreadsheet. The grader does not verify the exact range or colour — only that a matching rule exists. If the submit returns 0 (incorrect), the rule was not found: check that Conditional Formatting was applied (not just a manual fill colour) and that the value entered was 10000 without a comma or dollar sign.

_comment_GAMIFICATION: XP totals to 95 for a perfect run. Combo kicks in at 3-streak for 1.5x multiplier.
